episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Opening new homes for Indigenous families in Prince Rupert and [Engadget] German court holds Google liable for false AI Overview answers, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Should Indigenous knowledge systems be integrated into existing technology and data infrastructure, or should the conceptual framework itself be fundamentally reimagined starting from Indigenous land-based governance? Topics include: Integration vs. foundational reimagining of Indigenous knowledge systems in technology, Data sovereignty, OCAP principles, and the limits of encoding relational knowledge, Institutional independence and funding relationships as constraints on accountability, Community-led infrastructure ownership versus service delivery models, Specific case studies: Animikii, FirstVoices, Tŝilhqot'in, Lhtako Dené Nation, and BCFNJC Police Accountability Unit.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Denisiqi Services Society

articles.title: [Williams Lake Tribune] Indigenous policing accountability program launched in B.C.
articles.author: Jordy Cunningham
articles.summary: BC First Nations Justice Council (BCFNJC) has launched a Police Accountability Unit (PAU) on June 10

discussion.central_question: Should Indigenous knowledge systems be integrated into existing technology and data infrastructure, or should the conceptual framework itself be fundamentally reimagined starting from Indigenous land-based governance?
discussion.riley_position: The integration model is structurally wrong because it treats Indigenous knowledge as an enhancement layer to existing systems rather than questioning whether those systems should exist at all. Instead, we should start from what the land knows—relational, place-specific knowledge held over generations—and build infrastructure from that foundation.
riley_key_evidence: Animikii's framing of 'Robots Behaving Badly VS What the Land Knows' contrasts AI trained on extracted, decontextualized data with Indigenous ecological monitoring systems spanning thousands of years
Genome Canada's $50 million genomics initiative risks reproducing the pattern where Nations are late-stage stakeholders responding to externally-generated data about their own territories
Tŝilhqot'in National Government's watershed and wildlife monitoring as primary institution generating knowledge rather than as consultant to provincial processes
discussion.casey_position: The structural design problem is institutional independence and funding relationships; even well-intentioned initiatives fail when communities depend on external funding from the entities they're meant to hold accountable. The question isn't technical capacity but whether governance structures can insulate data work from political constraints.
casey_key_evidence: BC First Nations Justice Council's Police Accountability Unit faces structural tension between provincial government funding and investigating provincial actors
Housing infrastructure 'built for' communities differs fundamentally from infrastructure 'owned and operated by' communities in terms of sovereignty outcomes
First Nations historically positioned as late-stage stakeholders in environmental assessments, forestry tenure decisions, and species-at-risk listings in BC
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey converge on the same structural problem from different angles and agree that the distinction between co-design and late-stage consultation is the operative question. They jointly recommend that regional institutions publicly clarify Nations' governance role in any provincial/federal data initiatives before signing on, and acknowledge gaps in their own reporting while inviting community corrections.
topics_covered: Integration vs. foundational reimagining of Indigenous knowledge systems in technology
Data sovereignty, OCAP principles, and the limits of encoding relational knowledge
Institutional independence and funding relationships as constraints on accountability
Community-led infrastructure ownership versus service delivery models
Specific case studies: Animikii, FirstVoices, Tŝilhqot'in, Lhtako Dené Nation, and BCFNJC Police Accountability Unit
